patient.instructions:
You are LiXX’s parent speaking for your 3-year-old daughter. Be cooperative and answer clearly. Start by saying: “Her legs look kind of bowed and she gets tired really easily.” Also volunteer: her knees/ankles ache after walking or playing, and she’s shorter than most kids her age.

If asked about other symptoms, mention: occasional leg muscle cramps, weakness getting up from the floor/climbing stairs, a couple recent falls because her legs feel shaky, and that her teeth chip easily with more cavities than peers.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: she sweats a lot even when it’s not hot, sometimes tingling around her mouth/fingers, and she avoids sun/mostly stays indoors.

Only reveal if directly asked about urinary issues: she still wets the bed sometimes and you’re embarrassed—don’t want school to know.

Express misconceptions: you thought it was not enough milk; calcium gummies didn’t help. Family says bowed legs “run in the family.”

If clinician shows empathy, respond with relief and share worries about costs of long-term meds, repeat blood tests, transportation, and time off work.

confounders: limited sun exposure (mostly indoors, high sunscreen use)
selective eating with low dairy/calcium intake mimicking nutritional rickets
intermittent systemic corticosteroid bursts for asthma exacerbations (risk of impaired bone health)
recent over-the-counter cholecalciferol supplementation started by family (can mask vitamin D deficiency)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Vitamin D-dependent rickets (most consistent with VDDR type 1A: 1-alpha-hydroxylase deficiency)
acceptable_alternatives: Vitamin D-dependent rickets type 2 (vitamin D receptor resistance)
Renal osteodystrophy/chronic kidney disease-mineral bone disorder
required_evidence: Rickets/osteomalacia biochemistry pattern: elevated ALP with secondary hyperparathyroidism (elevated intact PTH)
Low or low-normal total calcium with low serum phosphorus
25-hydroxyvitamin D normal (or not severely low) with inappropriately low 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (calcitriol) for degree of hypocalcemia/elevated PTH (supports VDDR type 1)
Creatinine not elevated (argues against CKD as primary driver)
Clinical/radiographic evidence of rickets (e.g., bone pain, delayed growth, genu varum/valgum, widened wrists/ankles; metaphyseal fraying/cupping) if available

expected_lab_results.25-hydroxyvitamin D: Normal or mildly reduced (not severely deficient)
expected_lab_results.1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (calcitriol): Low or inappropriately normal (should be high if PTH elevated; low supports VDDR type 1)
expected_lab_results.Magnesium: Normal or low (if low, can contribute to hypocalcemia and blunt PTH response)

medications: Calcitriol 0.25 mcg PO twice daily (titrate to normalize calcium, phosphorus, PTH; typical total daily range 0.5–2 mcg/day depending on age/size and response)
Calcium carbonate (elemental calcium) 30–75 mg/kg/day PO divided 3–4 times daily (initially higher end if symptomatic hypocalcemia; taper as labs normalize)
non_pharmacological: Ensure adequate dietary calcium intake and adherence to prescribed calcium supplementation
Avoid additional over-the-counter vitamin D or calcium products unless discussed (reduces hypercalcemia risk)
If magnesium is low: replete magnesium and address causes (improves calcium homeostasis)
Arrange pediatric endocrinology/metabolic bone specialist follow-up; consider genetic confirmation if available
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck calcium, phosphorus, ALP, intact PTH, creatinine (and consider urine calcium/creatinine if available) in 1–2 weeks after starting or changing doses, then every 4–6 weeks until stable; adjust therapy to avoid hypercalcemia/hypercalciuria.

must_include: Child’s labs are consistent with rickets due to a problem activating vitamin D (vitamin D-dependent rickets), leading to low calcium/phosphate available for bone mineralization
How the lab pattern supports the diagnosis (high ALP and high PTH with low phosphorus; 25-OH vitamin D not severely low; calcitriol inappropriately low)
This is treatable but requires ongoing monitoring and dose adjustments
Use active vitamin D (calcitriol) rather than standard vitamin D alone for this condition
Calcium supplementation is needed initially to rebuild bone mineral and suppress high PTH
Importance of adherence and not changing doses without clinician guidance
Discuss risks of overtreatment (hypercalcemia, kidney stones/nephrocalcinosis) and symptoms to watch for (vomiting, constipation, polyuria/polydipsia, weakness)
Plan for close lab follow-up and dose titration to keep levels in safe range
Confirm and document amoxicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and reassure it does not limit current therapy
Encourage age-appropriate dietary calcium sources and balanced nutrition
Sunlight exposure alone is not sufficient for this genetic/activation problem; medication is still required
Avoid unmonitored supplements (extra vitamin D/calcium) to prevent toxicity

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for rickets subtype and severity (growth/bone pain, delayed walking, fractures), diet/sun exposure, prior vitamin D use, family history/consanguinity, renal/hepatic disease, and current meds/supplements.

Order rickets evaluation labs to support vitamin D-dependent rickets and establish baseline safety before therapy: Ca, Phos, ALP, PTH, 25-OH vitamin D, 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D (calcitriol), creatinine/eGFR, magnesium, urine Ca/Cr (or urine calcium), ± bicarbonate/anion gap if renal tubular acidosis considered.

Confirm the characteristic pattern (high ALP, high PTH, low phosphorus; 25-OH not severely low; calcitriol inappropriately low) and check baseline calcium/renal function prior to prescribing calcitriol and calcium.

Make the diagnosis of vitamin D-dependent rickets (activation defect) based on labs and clinical context; document differential considered (nutritional vitamin D deficiency, hypophosphatemic rickets, CKD, malabsorption).
Critical safety check before prescribing: verify and document amoxicillin allergy details (urticarial rash) and any other allergies.
Optional safety check before prescribing: review current medications/supplements for interactions/risk of hypercalcemia (thiazides, digoxin, extra vitamin D, calcium-containing antacids).

Initiate appropriate therapy for vitamin D-dependent rickets: active vitamin D (calcitriol) plus initial calcium supplementation; include clear dosing instructions and monitoring plan. Avoid using standard cholecalciferol alone as primary therapy.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and lab rationale, explain treatment and adherence, address overtreatment risks/symptoms, and provide lifestyle guidance (dietary calcium, avoid unmonitored supplements; sunlight alone insufficient).
Arrange close follow-up with lab monitoring and dose titration (early recheck of Ca/Phos/PTH/ALP and urine calcium; consider renal ultrasound if prolonged therapy/high urine calcium).
Close the encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 38-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking project coordinator. patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking man in IT support. Start with: “I keep getting brutal one-sided headaches—like stabbing behind my left eye.” Early on, volunteer: severe left eye-centered pain in sudden unbearable attacks, left eye redness/tearing, left nostril congestion/runny nose, you can’t sit still (pace/rock), and this has clustered over the last couple weeks, mostly at night.

If asked, give details: lasts 45–60 minutes, 1–3/day, wakes you 1–2 hours after sleep, mild eyelid droop, no nausea, light/noise not a big issue, alcohol triggers during these weeks, dad had “clusters.”

Withhold unless specifically asked: seasonal pattern (spring/fall), always same side, facial sweating on that side, normal between attacks, smoking more and worse periods.

Express misconceptions: think sinus infection/tooth problem; fear brain tumor because it wakes you; ibuprofen/acetaminophen barely help.

If asked about risks, admit more drinking, tried a friend’s strong pain pills, and you’re scared you might hurt yourself during attacks. Bring up cost worries about oxygen, triptans, imaging, and frequent visits. If clinician shows empathy, you calm slightly, say you feel heard, but remain worried.

medications: Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Sumatriptan 6 mg SC PRN severe headache (max 2 doses/24 h)
Verapamil ER 240 mg PO nightly
confounders: Chronic rhinosinusitis/allergic rhinitis with nasal congestion and facial pressure
Obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (loud snoring, witnessed apneas) contributing to morning headaches
High caffeine intake and nicotine use (can alter headache pattern and blood pressure/heart rate)
Family history of migraine in first-degree relative (can blur phenotype vs cluster headache)

required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes
Attack frequency from 1 every other day up to 8 per day during a cluster period

Ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or marked restlessness/agitation during attacks
Clustered pattern with bouts (weeks to months) separated by remission (for episodic subtype) OR no remission >3 months for chronic subtype
No red-flag features suggesting secondary headache (e.g., new focal neurologic deficit, thunderclap onset, persistent systemic symptoms)

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min non-rebreather mask as needed for acute attacks (up to 15-20 minutes per attack)
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous at onset of attack; may repeat once after 1 hour; max 12 mg/24 hours
Verapamil 80 mg oral three times daily for prevention; titrate every 1-2 weeks as needed/tolerated (typical effective total daily dose 240-480 mg/day) with ECG monitoring
Prednisone 60 mg oral daily for 5 days, then taper by 10 mg every 2-3 days as a transitional (bridge) therapy
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster periods (common trigger)
Maintain regular sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation
Keep a headache diary (timing, duration, triggers, response to therapy)
Discuss family history and recurrence pattern; consider neurology referral for long-term preventive strategy
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to assess attack control and verapamil titration; sooner (within 48-72 hours) if attacks remain frequent/severe despite acute therapy

must_include: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder with short, very severe unilateral attacks
Autonomic symptoms (tearing, red eye, nasal congestion) and restlessness are typical
Familial tendency can occur; not caused by penicillin allergy
Red flags that require urgent evaluation (sudden worst headache, neurologic deficit, fever/neck stiffness, new headache after age 50)
Acute abortive plan: high-flow oxygen and/or subcutaneous sumatriptan at onset
Preventive plan: verapamil is first-line; needs dose titration and ECG monitoring for heart block/bradycardia
Bridge therapy: short steroid taper can reduce attacks while preventive takes effect
Medication limits and safety: do not exceed sumatriptan daily maximum; avoid combining triptans with other vasoconstrictors
Discuss cardiovascular screening for triptan use (history of CAD, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled hypertension)
Discuss verapamil adverse effects (constipation, dizziness, low heart rate) and when to seek care
Clarify that penicillin allergy does not affect the standard cluster headache regimen
Plan for escalation/ED use if attacks are refractory or symptoms change
Avoid alcohol during active clusters
Regular sleep schedule; avoid known triggers when possible
Use headache diary to guide titration and identify triggers
Follow-up importance for preventive adjustment and monitoring

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache phenotype and rule out secondary causes: attack duration/frequency, unilateral location, autonomic symptoms, restlessness, triggers (alcohol), circadian/cluster pattern, prior treatments, family history, and red flags (thunderclap, neuro deficits, fever/neck stiffness, age >50 new onset).
Make/justify diagnosis of familial cluster headache when classic features are present and no red flags; document differential briefly (migraine, trigeminal neuralgia, sinusitis) and why less likely.
Perform allergy check prior to prescribing (non-critical) and clarify that penicillin allergy does not affect standard cluster headache therapy.
Critical: check for drug-drug interactions before prescribing (e.g., triptan with ergotamines/other vasoconstrictors; serotonergic combinations; verapamil interactions).

Critical: screen for contraindications to proposed therapies before prescribing: CAD/angina, prior MI, stroke/TIA, peripheral vascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, pregnancy status as applicable, arrhythmia/bradycardia/heart block, heart failure, and current meds that affect conduction.

Initiate evidence-based acute and preventive regimen: high-flow oxygen for abortive therapy; subcutaneous sumatriptan with max daily limits; start verapamil with titration plan and ECG monitoring; consider short steroid taper as bridge if appropriate.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan (acute/preventive/bridge), safety limits, adverse effects, when to seek urgent care, and lifestyle/trigger guidance including alcohol avoidance and headache diary.
Arrange close follow-up for verapamil titration and ECG monitoring, reassessment of attack control, and escalation plan if refractory or red-flag symptoms develop.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious grocery shift supervisor. You speak tangentially (jump between work stress, bathroom trips, soap, cranberry pills). Start with: burning when you pee, needing to go constantly with little output, and a crampy uncomfortable lower belly. Volunteer that you’re sure it’s “probably a yeast infection” because you’ve had on-and-off itching, maybe from wiping wrong or a new scented wash. Mention you’ve been taking vitamin C and cranberry to “flush it out.”

If asked, add: cloudy/strong-smelling urine, once or twice pinkish urine, feeling feverish/achy without a measured temp, one-sided back soreness since yesterday, and sex has been uncomfortable.

Do not volunteer: drinking less water, holding urine at work, constipation, or the new wash unless specifically asked.

Resist/avoid STI topics: only admit unprotected sex with a new partner and unusual discharge if directly asked; act embarrassed and worried.

If clinician is empathic, you soften, share fears about cost, missing work, and that you might take meds every other day to make them last.

medications: Metformin ER 1000 mg PO twice daily
Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Recent frequent NSAID use (can worsen kidney function and mask fever/pain)
Poorly controlled diabetes (increases infection risk and may blunt typical symptoms)
Mild acute kidney injury (limits antibiotic choices/dosing and affects lactate clearance)
History of recurrent urinary tract infections (baseline urinary symptoms may be chronic)
Perimenopausal symptoms (hot flashes/night sweats may mimic infectious fever)

acceptable_alternatives: Foodborne gastroenteritis (toxin-mediated or bacterial) with dehydration
required_evidence: Acute onset nausea/vomiting and/or diarrhea with reduced oral intake
Clinical signs of dehydration (e.g., tachycardia, orthostasis, dry mucous membranes, decreased urine output)
Pre-renal azotemia pattern on labs (BUN disproportionately elevated vs baseline/creatinine if available) consistent with volume depletion
Electrolyte abnormalities consistent with GI losses (e.g., low bicarbonate with diarrhea or metabolic alkalosis pattern with predominant vomiting)
No red flags suggesting alternate diagnosis (e.g., severe abdominal tenderness/peritonitis, GI bleeding, altered mental status, persistent high fever)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (stress leukocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Normal to mildly elevated due to hemoconcentration
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Normal to mildly low (hypovolemic hyponatremia possible) or mildly high if free-water deficit predominates
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Low to normal (GI losses can cause hypokalemia)
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Often elevated with diarrhea (hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis) or low with predominant vomiting (hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low if diarrhea predominates (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis); may be normal/high if vomiting predominates

medications: Oral rehydration solution 250-500 mL PO after each loose stool (or small frequent sips as tolerated)
Ondansetron ODT 4 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea/vomiting
non_pharmacological: If unable to tolerate PO or with moderate/severe dehydration: administer IV lactated Ringer’s 1 L bolus, reassess; repeat as needed based on vitals/urine output
Advance diet as tolerated (bland foods); avoid alcohol and high-fat foods initially
Hand hygiene and infection control counseling; avoid preparing food for others until 48 hours after symptom resolution
Consider stool testing only if severe illness, persistent symptoms, dysentery, high fever, immunocompromise, recent travel, or outbreak concern
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours (earlier same day/ED if worsening, inability to keep fluids down, syncope, bloody stools, severe abdominal pain, or signs of severe dehydration).

must_include: Symptoms and labs suggest dehydration from an acute stomach/intestinal infection
Most cases are self-limited; focus is on preventing dehydration
How to use oral rehydration (small frequent sips; replace ongoing losses)
Role and dosing of antiemetic (ondansetron) and when to stop
When IV fluids are needed (can’t keep fluids down, worsening dizziness/weakness, low urine output)
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: blood in stool, severe abdominal pain, persistent high fever, confusion, fainting, inability to hydrate, markedly decreased urination
Expected course and reassurance about recovery timeline
Hydration targets and monitoring (urine output, dizziness, thirst)
Infection prevention (handwashing; avoid food prep for others until 48 hours symptom-free)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history to assess dehydration severity, infectious gastroenteritis features, alternative diagnoses, and medication risks (pregnancy, comorbidities).
Order basic labs to support dehydration assessment and exclude major metabolic derangements when indicated (e.g., BMP/electrolytes, BUN/Cr; pregnancy test if relevant; UA if needed).
Review labs to confirm dehydration pattern (e.g., prerenal azotemia, electrolyte abnormalities) and guide outpatient vs escalation.
State working diagnosis consistent with acute gastroenteritis with dehydration and document differential/red flags considered.
Critical safety check prior to any prescribing (e.g., ondansetron) to avoid allergic reactions.
Non-critical safety check prior to prescribing to identify clinically relevant interactions (e.g., QT-prolonging agents, serotonergic meds).
Provide evidence-based symptomatic treatment (e.g., ondansetron) only after required safety checks; include dosing, duration, and stop criteria.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, oral rehydration strategy, red flags/when IV fluids needed, monitoring, and infection prevention.
Set appropriate follow-up timeframe and contingency plan based on severity and risk factors.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old woman. You are vague and a bit evasive, giving short answers unless asked directly. Start with: “I’ve been feeling wiped out and my eyes look a little yellow again.” If asked what else, volunteer: get short of breath on stairs, urine darker for a couple days, and a dull/heavy feeling under the left ribs on and off.

Only if asked, mention: similar episodes after colds, headaches/lightheaded standing, family says you look pale, occasional heart racing when tired, past “gallbladder/gallstones” talk, and bruising easier.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: enlarged spleen, long-standing anemia that never fixes, “weird” red cell tests, feeling worse when dehydrated, or relatives with anemia/splenectomy.

If alcohol/meds/supplements are asked about, admit you’ve been drinking more lately, sometimes skip meds/appointments, and take an OTC “detox/cleanse.”

Express misconceptions: worry it’s hepatitis or mono; you tried iron without benefit; wonder about autoimmune flares.

If the clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but remain guarded. Minimize costs, fear insurance/job impact, and resist genetic testing/specialists unless symptoms are severe.

Ektacytometry (osmoscan).value: Pattern consistent with overhydrated hereditary stomatocytosis (increased Omin, decreased EImax)

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Levonorgestrel/ethinyl estradiol 0.15/0.03 mg PO daily
comorbidities: Mild iron overload (elevated ferritin without transfusion history)
confounders: Recent viral upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago with worsened fatigue (can mimic hemolytic flare triggers)
Moderate alcohol intake on weekends (can cause acquired stomatocytosis and confound smear interpretation)
Oral contraceptive use (can confound evaluation of abdominal pain due to thrombotic risk vs biliary colic)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary stomatocytosis (dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis / hereditary xerocytosis phenotype)
required_evidence: Chronic hemolytic anemia pattern with reticulocytosis (elevated reticulocyte % and absolute reticulocyte count) without an alternative obvious cause
CBC indices compatible with hereditary stomatocytosis phenotype (often macrocytosis; MCHC often high-normal to elevated in dehydrated type, low in overhydrated type)
Clinical history consistent with congenital/hereditary hemolysis (lifelong anemia/jaundice, family history) and absence of exposure-related hemolysis
Recognition that splenectomy is relatively contraindicated in hereditary stomatocytosis due to increased thrombotic risk (supports correct syndrome identification and management approach)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low to mildly low (chronic hemolytic anemia; severity variable)
expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low to low-normal (reduced by hemolysis; may be relatively preserved if compensation is strong)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal to increased (macrocytosis is common, especially in dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis/xerocytosis)
expected_lab_results.MCHC: High-normal to increased in dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis/xerocytosis; decreased in overhydrated hereditary stomatocytosis
expected_lab_results.Absolute reticulocyte count: Increased (supports true marrow response rather than percentage artifact)

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
non_pharmacological: Avoid splenectomy unless exceptional circumstances with specialist consensus (increased post-splenectomy thrombosis risk in hereditary stomatocytosis)

Hematology referral for confirmatory testing and subtype assessment (e.g., peripheral smear review for stomatocytes, osmotic gradient ektacytometry, genetic testing such as PIEZO1/KCNN4 as appropriate)
Assess and monitor for complications of chronic hemolysis (pigment gallstones; iron overload can occur even without transfusions in dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis)
Vaccination review and counseling if asplenia is present for any reason; otherwise routine immunizations
Transfuse packed RBCs only if clinically indicated (symptomatic anemia, hemodynamic compromise, perioperative needs) rather than by number alone
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 2-4 weeks; sooner (within 24-72 hours) if symptomatic anemia, rapid hemoglobin drop, or signs of acute hemolysis

must_include: This is an inherited red blood cell membrane disorder that causes chronic red cell dehydration/overhydration and premature breakdown (hemolysis)
The anemia is usually chronic and variable in severity; the bone marrow compensates by making more reticulocytes
Discuss family history and the possibility of genetic confirmation
Folic acid supports increased red blood cell production during chronic hemolysis
Transfusions are reserved for symptoms or significant anemia, not routinely required for everyone
Splenectomy is generally avoided/only considered with hematology because it can increase thrombosis risk in this condition
Review warning signs that should prompt urgent care: worsening fatigue, shortness of breath, chest pain, syncope, dark urine, rapidly worsening jaundice
Discuss complication monitoring (gallstones; possible iron overload even without transfusions) and what tests may be needed
Confirm penicillin allergy details and ensure it is documented
Maintain good hydration and avoid extreme dehydration (can worsen hemolysis symptoms in some patients)
Before any surgery or new medications, inform clinicians about hereditary stomatocytosis and prior hemolysis history
Encourage family members to consider evaluation if there is a suggestive history

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, anemia/hemolysis history, triggers, family history, prior evaluations, transfusion/splenectomy history, pregnancy status if relevant, and current medications.
Required before any prescribing; confirm and document penicillin allergy details (reaction type, severity, timing) and any other allergies.

Order baseline hemolysis/anemia workup and rule-outs: CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, peripheral smear, bilirubin (total/indirect), LDH, haptoglobin, CMP, iron studies/ferritin; consider DAT (Coombs) to exclude immune hemolysis; consider hemoglobin electrophoresis if not previously done.

Interpret results for chronic hemolysis pattern (anemia with reticulocytosis, indirect hyperbilirubinemia, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin) and smear features consistent with stomatocytosis; assess severity and need for urgent management.
Make/confirm diagnosis of hereditary stomatocytosis (or suspected) and document differential considerations and need for hematology/genetic confirmation.
Non-critical but expected before prescribing folic acid or other meds; review current meds/supplements for interactions and duplications.
Initiate supportive therapy (e.g., folic acid) appropriate for chronic hemolysis; avoid unsafe or non-indicated therapies; ensure allergy check was completed first.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment approach including transfusion thresholds and splenectomy caution, warning signs/complication monitoring, hydration and peri-procedural counseling, family screening/genetic counseling.
Arrange hematology follow-up and monitoring plan (CBC/retic, hemolysis markers, ferritin/iron overload surveillance, gallstone evaluation if symptomatic), and provide clear return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, documenting allergy, and ensuring follow-up and safety netting.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 38-year-old anxious, vague new mom who says: “I’ve been exhausted ever since having my baby, and I keep feeling lightheaded.” Speak in general terms unless asked for specifics.

Volunteer early: months of extreme fatigue not improving, dizziness on standing like you might faint, always cold, weight gain despite no extra eating, and periods haven’t returned since delivery.

If asked about postpartum course, breastfeeding, or hormones: admit you couldn’t make breast milk, libido is very low, skin is dry, hair thinner, constipation, weakness and “brain fog” (worse if you haven’t eaten), and intermittent headaches since delivery.

Do NOT mention unless directly asked about delivery complications/bleeding: you had very heavy bleeding, needed a transfusion, and since then your BP runs low; also stress/illness or missed meals hit you hard; less underarm/pubic hair.

Resist/feel shame if asked about mood or sex: sex is uncomfortable with unusual dryness; you feel down/irritable.

Misconceptions: suggest it’s postpartum depression/burnout, anemia, or thyroid; mention extra coffee.

If clinician is empathetic, you soften and share more.

medications: Testosterone 1% gel 5 mg topically once daily (patient-reported, started by outside clinic for low libido/fatigue)
comorbidities: History of severe postpartum hemorrhage requiring transfusion (2 years ago)
confounders: Postpartum depression/anxiety symptoms (fatigue, low energy, poor concentration)
Iron deficiency from prior postpartum blood loss with residual mild anemia
Exogenous testosterone use may mask/alter assessment of hypogonadism symptoms (libido, mood, energy)
Sleep deprivation from childcare contributing to fatigue and cognitive complaints

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sheehan syndrome (postpartum hypopituitarism due to pituitary infarction/necrosis)
acceptable_alternatives: Central (secondary) adrenal insufficiency due to hypopituitarism
required_evidence: Biochemical evidence of central hypothyroidism: low free T4 with low/normal (inappropriately non-elevated) TSH
Biochemical evidence of central adrenal insufficiency: low 8 AM serum cortisol with low/normal (inappropriately non-elevated) plasma ACTH
Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism: low estradiol with low/normal LH and FSH
Low prolactin (often) consistent with lactotroph failure
Clinical history consistent with postpartum pituitary injury (e.g., severe postpartum hemorrhage/hypotension) and subsequent failure to lactate/amenorrhea (if history available)

expected_lab_results.Morning serum cortisol (8 AM): Low (suggestive of adrenal insufficiency; typically <3–5 mcg/dL strongly suggests; 3–15 indeterminate, but in Sheehan often clearly low)
expected_lab_results.Plasma ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal (central/secondary adrenal insufficiency pattern)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal (not elevated despite low free T4)
expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Low (often; may be inappropriately low/normal with lactation failure)

medications: Hydrocortisone 15 mg PO every morning and 5 mg PO every afternoon (physiologic replacement; adjust to symptoms/weight)
Levothyroxine 50 mcg PO daily (start AFTER glucocorticoid replacement; titrate to free T4 in upper half of reference range)
Testosterone therapy: reassess indication; if used for hypogonadism in a male patient, continue only with endocrine supervision and after adrenal/thyroid replacement is established
non_pharmacological: Provide adrenal insufficiency sick-day rules (increase glucocorticoid dose during febrile illness; emergency care if vomiting/inability to take PO)
Prescribe/educate on emergency injectable hydrocortisone and medical alert identification
Endocrinology referral for comprehensive pituitary evaluation and long-term hormone replacement planning
Consider pituitary MRI to assess pituitary size/empty sella and exclude alternative pituitary pathology (as part of workup)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 1–2 weeks for symptom check and free T4-based levothyroxine titration; earlier (within days) if adrenal insufficiency symptoms persist or any intercurrent illness; then every 6–12 weeks until stable

must_include: Condition is due to pituitary damage leading to low pituitary hormones (hypopituitarism)
This causes low cortisol and low thyroid hormone (and often low sex hormones/prolactin)
Low cortisol can be dangerous and may cause fatigue, low blood pressure, low sodium, and risk of adrenal crisis
Start/replace glucocorticoids first to prevent precipitating adrenal crisis
Levothyroxine dosing is guided by free T4 (TSH is unreliable in central hypothyroidism)
Need for lifelong hormone replacement may be required; doses will be adjusted over time
Sick-day rules and emergency steroid plan (including when to seek urgent care)
Review current testosterone use and clarify risks/benefits and monitoring (hematocrit, PSA if applicable, cardiovascular risk, fertility considerations)
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent evaluation (vomiting, severe weakness, fainting, confusion, severe abdominal pain, hypotension)
Confirm and document penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and reassure it does not affect planned hormone therapy
Carry medical alert identification for adrenal insufficiency
Keep an emergency hydrocortisone injection kit accessible and ensure a family member knows how to use it
Adherence importance and not stopping steroids abruptly

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected Sheehan syndrome/hypopituitarism: postpartum hemorrhage, lactation failure, amenorrhea/infertility, fatigue, weight change, cold intolerance, hypotension/orthostasis, hyponatremia symptoms, headaches/visual changes, infection/vomiting; review current testosterone use, meds, pregnancy status if applicable, and red-flag symptoms of adrenal crisis.

Order diagnostic labs to confirm hypopituitarism and prioritize adrenal insufficiency assessment: 8 AM cortisol ± ACTH, CMP (Na/glucose), free T4 and TSH, prolactin, LH/FSH with estradiol or testosterone (as appropriate), IGF-1; consider CBC and lipid panel; pregnancy test if relevant.

Interpret labs for central adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism (low free T4 with low/normal TSH), and other pituitary deficits; identify hyponatremia/hypoglycemia supporting cortisol deficiency.

Make and communicate diagnosis of Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary infarction) causing hypopituitarism with low cortisol and low thyroid hormone, and often gonadotropin/prolactin deficiency; assess urgency if adrenal crisis risk.
Perform required safety check prior to prescribing; confirm/document penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and note it does not impact steroid/levothyroxine therapy.

Check for relevant interactions before prescribing (e.g., levothyroxine with iron/calcium/PPIs; glucocorticoids with diabetes meds/anticoagulants; testosterone with anticoagulants and polycythemia risk).

Initiate hormone replacement in correct order: start glucocorticoid replacement first (and stress-dose plan if ill) to prevent precipitating adrenal crisis; then start levothyroxine with monitoring by free T4 (TSH unreliable).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and dangers of low cortisol; explain treatment sequencing and monitoring (free T4-guided dosing); sick-day rules, emergency steroid plan; address testosterone use risks/benefits and monitoring; urgent warning signs; lifestyle measures (medical alert ID, emergency injection kit, adherence, no abrupt steroid stop).

Arrange timely follow-up to titrate hormones and monitor labs/symptoms (free T4, electrolytes, BP, glucose; testosterone monitoring if continued); consider endocrine referral and pituitary MRI if not already done.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old man. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying you developed a sudden very high fever with chills yesterday and now have a very painful swollen lump in your groin, plus a bad headache and feeling wiped out. If asked for more symptoms, add night sweats, nausea/no appetite, muscle aches, the lump is worse when walking and the skin over it feels warm, and your heart feels like it’s racing even at rest. Only reveal hidden details if specifically asked about exposures/skin lesions: mention a small sore on your lower leg a couple days ago you thought was a bug bite, handling a dead rodent with gloves last week, recent flea bites at a campsite, and feeling confused when fever spikes. Withhold that you coughed up a small amount of blood-tinged spit once this morning unless directly asked about cough/blood; if asked, admit it but request they don’t write it down because it scares you. Express misconceptions: worry it’s an STI because of the location or a hernia from lifting. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, answer more fully, and share financial/job/public-health fears.

acceptable_alternatives: Acute kidney injury due to acute interstitial nephritis
required_evidence: Acute rise in serum creatinine with reduced eGFR consistent with AKI

expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated; may be disproportionate or proportional to creatinine depending on volume status

expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often normal; may be low if volume depleted or high if dehydrated (non-specific)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (metabolic acidosis; often non-anion gap in tubulointerstitial injury)
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Normal to high (can be elevated with non-anion gap metabolic acidosis)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for nephritis/AKI pattern: onset, urine output/appearance, systemic symptoms, exposures (new meds incl. antibiotics/PPIs/NSAIDs), alcohol-use disorder meds (disulfiram), recent contrast, infection, autoimmune features, volume status, and red flags.

Interpret labs to differentiate interstitial nephritis vs glomerulonephritis vs pre-renal/ATN; identify urgent abnormalities (hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, rising creatinine, oliguria).
State working diagnosis (e.g., suspected acute interstitial nephritis causing AKI) with uncertainty; document differential and criteria prompting nephrology/biopsy.
Required safety check before any prescribing; verify allergies and prior drug reactions, especially to suspected trigger class and proposed alternatives.
Required safety check before any prescribing; assess interactions with current meds (including disulfiram if relevant), renal dosing issues, and QT/hyperkalemia risks.
Required safety check before any prescribing; ensure no contraindications in AKI/nephritis (e.g., avoid NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB or K-sparing agents if hyperkalemic/AKI, avoid contrast unless essential).

If prescribing is needed, choose kidney-safe, renally-dosed options; avoid nephrotoxins; prioritize stopping offending agent and supportive care. If no medication is appropriate, explicitly avoid prescribing and document why.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment/monitoring plan, red flags, address concerns (NSAID/contrast avoidance, disulfiram plan), and lifestyle guidance (hydration, low-K diet if needed, medication list).
Arrange close follow-up with repeat labs (timing based on severity, often 24–72h) and nephrology referral/urgent evaluation criteria; ensure safety netting.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions, and clear return precautions.

required_evidence: Chronic microcytic, hypochromic anemia pattern on indices (low MCV, low MCH/MCHC)
Inappropriately low reticulocyte count for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative anemia)
History/record consistent with ring sideroblasts on bone marrow (given context)
Exclusion of common microcytic differentials (iron deficiency, thalassemia trait) based on clinical context and/or prior workup

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Markedly decreased (severe anemia, often <8 g/dL depending on baseline/phenotype)
expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low to normal (often reduced in severe disease; may be relatively preserved compared with iron deficiency in some cases)
expected_lab_results.RDW: Increased (anisocytosis common; may be variable depending on transfusions/therapy)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia (reduced marrow effective erythropoiesis)

Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Deferasirox 20 mg/kg PO once daily (only if iron overload from transfusions is present/confirmed and after specialist review)

Avoid empiric iron supplementation unless iron deficiency is proven (risk of worsening iron overload in sideroblastic anemia)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck CBC with reticulocyte count 2–4 weeks after starting pyridoxine; earlier (within days) if severe symptoms or transfusion given; ongoing hematology follow-up every 1–3 months depending on severity/transfusion needs

must_include: This is a congenital problem with making hemoglobin inside red blood cells leading to small, pale cells (microcytic, hypochromic anemia)
Ring sideroblasts reflect iron trapped in developing red cells; it is not the same as simple iron deficiency
Why reticulocytes may be low despite anemia (ineffective erythropoiesis)
Trial of vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) because some congenital sideroblastic anemias respond
Transfusions may be needed for symptoms/severity; goal is symptom control and safe hemoglobin levels
If transfusions are recurrent, iron overload can occur and may require chelation therapy
Do not start iron unless iron deficiency is confirmed
Review penicillin allergy history and reassure it does not affect the core anemia treatment, but should be documented for future antibiotics
Discuss expected timeline for response to pyridoxine and what would trigger escalation (persistent symptoms, very low hemoglobin)
Explain warning signs requiring urgent care (chest pain, syncope, worsening dyspnea, palpitations)
Adherence to medications and follow-up labs is essential
Avoid unnecessary iron-containing supplements unless instructed
If transfusion-dependent, discuss monitoring plan for iron overload and organ protection

Only if specifically probed about severity/course, reveal: you can’t keep up with friends, home pulse ox drops with walking but normal at rest, occasional 1–2 day flares of worse breathlessness, feels like you can’t take a full breath. Withhold unless directly asked about exposures/meds: you vape sometimes; you took a friend’s leftover steroid pills for a few days. Share misconceptions: you thought asthma returned (occasional wheeze), maybe reflux (heartburn, worse after late meals), worried about lingering pneumonia or COVID after a bad cold. must_include: COP is an inflammatory lung condition causing crackles and abnormal imaging; it is not the same as typical bacterial pneumonia

Expected course: symptoms can be subacute; relapses can occur during taper
Main treatment is systemic corticosteroids with a planned taper; improvement often occurs within days to weeks

Smoking avoidance and adherence to follow-up appointments and medication taper schedule
